pattern: Sorting the usage limits ascending and greedily trying to close out the next strictly-larger group as soon as enough total capacity has accumulated is optimal, because saving unused capacity for a bigger group later never helps more than locking in a group now.
hints: The order you consume usage limits in doesn't matter for feasibility, only how much total capacity you have accumulated so far -- so sort the limits first.
Walk the sorted limits left to right, keeping a running total of unused capacity; the next group only needs one more unit of capacity than the previous group had.

As soon as the running total reaches the size the next group would need (previous group count + 1), commit to forming that group: increase the group count and subtract the new group's size from the running total, then keep scanning.

edge_inputs.description: three distinct limits allowing three increasing groups
unsorted input with a repeated limit
single number, maximum usage limit -- still only one group possible
four numbers each usable once still only support two groups total
reused numbers across groups thanks to a higher shared usage limit
a scarce number paired with an abundant one

stress.description: large random usage-limit array across the full value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    usageLimits = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [usageLimits]
